chain_of_thought: Top native frame is Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum inside libUbikeyJni.so, which appears to be application-level certificate/ASN.1 parsing code rather than an allocator or sanitizer; stack shows it called from Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → CERT_Load → Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage → fuzz_one_input → main.

Tools failed to decompile native functions due to backend 404 errors, so I cannot inspect the exact implementation of Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, its use of memcpy/memmove, or perform precise backward data-flow. However, naming strongly suggests it traverses ASN.1 structure children, likely based on length and type fields parsed from untrusted certificate data.

The JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage is known (from Jadx metadata) to have signature jGetKeyUsage(byte[]):String, so Java passes a byte[] into native that presumably contains an X.509 certificate or DER-encoded payload. Under normal app usage, that byte[] will come from the network, files, or external token, i.e., attacker-controllable data.

The fuzzer harness fuzz_one_input likely maps its input directly to that byte[]. The crash in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum when fuzzing suggests the function does not robustly validate ASN.1 lengths/child indices before using them, leading to invalid memory access or internal assertion failures while computing child numbers.

Even though I cannot see the exact unsafe operation, the combination of (1) a complex parser (ASN.1/X.509), (2) crash triggered by untrusted input, and (3) function role (computing child index/offset) makes it highly plausible that this is an out-of-bounds read/write or similar memory safety issue, which is classically exploitable in many ASN.1 implementations.

Because the JNI accepts raw byte[] without visible Java-side bounds checks beyond array length, and all parsing/validation seems to be done in native code, malformed external input could trigger the same condition in production, making this a genuine vulnerability rather than a harness-only artifact.

I lack symbol-level crash reason (SIGSEGV vs abort) and cannot confirm if safety checks abort with a controlled error (benign DoS) or if there is actual memory corruption. Thus I classify it as likely memory-safety vulnerability with medium–high confidence, and severity high (potential code execution / at least robust DoS).
reasons: Crash occurs in application ASN.1 parsing code (Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum) rather than in harness or allocator.
Native call chain is reached through a JNI method that takes a raw byte[] from Java, so attacker-controlled certificate data can reach the parser.
Function name and context imply computation of ASN.1 child offsets/counts, a classic source of out-of-bounds access when length/structure values are not fully validated.
Crash is triggered directly by fuzzed input with no evidence of prior strict validation, suggesting malformed ASN.1 can drive unsafe behavior in native code.
recommendations: Manually decompile and audit Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, Convert_ASN1_to_X509_* and CERT_Load for unchecked length/index arithmetic and pointer use.
Add strict bounds and structural validation for all ASN.1 fields (lengths, tags, child counts) before using them to compute offsets or child indices.
Implement robust error handling that rejects malformed ASN.1 data without accessing out-of-bounds memory, and add unit/fuzz tests around certificate parsing paths.
assumptions: jGetKeyUsage(byte[]) is reachable with attacker-supplied certificate data in real app usage (e.g., from network or files).
The crash reflects an invalid memory access or structural failure in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum rather than a deliberate, safe abort in a defensive check.
No strong validation of ASN.1 structure occurs in Java before invoking the JNI method.
limitations: Native functions could not be decompiled due to tool 404 errors, so exact unsafe instruction and memory access pattern are unknown.
Signal type and low-level crash details (e.g., registers, fault address) are not provided, limiting precision of the vulnerability classification.
The Java-side call paths into UbikeyJni beyond jGetKeyUsage signature are not fully visible, so exact source of certificate bytes is inferred.

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying a maliciously crafted X.509/ASN.1 byte[] to UbikeyJni.jGetKeyUsage via the app's certificate-handling feature
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to process arbitrary or partially controlled certificate/ASN.1 blobs (e.g., via network, QR code, or imported file).
Target device runs a vulnerable version of the app with the affected libUbikeyJni.so.
exploit_pipeline: Craft a DER-encoded certificate/ASN.1 blob with malformed length/child fields designed to stress Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum.
Deliver this blob through the app feature that eventually calls UbikeyJni.jGetKeyUsage(byte[]), causing Java to pass it to native code.
Trigger certificate parsing so CERT_Load invokes Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT and then Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum on attacker data.
Observe crash or undefined behavior; with further analysis, shape the payload to gain more controlled memory corruption, potentially enabling code execution.
poc_commands: adb shell am start -n <package>/<activity> --es cert_path /sdcard/Download/crafted_cert.der # assumes activity that loads a certificate from provided path
exploit.notes: Precise exploitation would require reverse engineering Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum to understand how ASN.1 fields map to memory offsets/indices and then tailoring DER fields to influence those computations predictably.

chain_of_thought: Stack trace shows abort → __stack_chk_fail → unknown frame → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → CERT_Load → Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage → fuzz_one_input → main. __stack_chk_fail indicates stack canary corruption, which is classic memory corruption (likely stack buffer overflow or similar) in or below Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT.

The first meaningful app-level native frame below the runtime is Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT inside libUbikeyJni.so. Direct code inspection via tools failed (404), so I must reason from naming, call chain, and crash type rather than exact C code. Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT suggests parsing arbitrary ASN.1 data into X.509 TBS certificate structures, commonly involving complex length parsing and nested fields. Such code often uses stack-allocated temporaries and memcpy-like operations and is historically prone to overflows.

The termination reason is __stack_chk_fail, which is emitted when the stack canary guard is overwritten at function epilogue. That means some write in the frame (or below) wrote past bounds. This is not a harness-only abrupt abort or assertion; it’s direct compiler-provided protection tripping due to memory corruption.

Fuzzer entry is fuzz_one_input, and the call path reaches JNI: fuzz_one_input → Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage → CERT_Load → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT. The harness is likely providing arbitrary certificate/ASN.1 bytes into jGetKeyUsage. In normal app operation, those bytes will come from untrusted sources (e.g., a remote server’s certificate or a smartcard/USB token) which is under at least partial attacker control. That is consistent with realistic attacker influence on the parsed ASN.1 data.

Even though I cannot inspect Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT, the combination of: (1) function name indicating complex parsing of external ASN.1, (2) stack canary failure, and (3) lack of any indication that the harness is doing something impossible (like passing null where production never would) strongly suggests an out-of-bounds write on the stack based on parsed ASN.1 lengths. This is exactly the pattern of typical, exploitable parsing bugs in certificate libraries.

I need to determine whether to treat this as a real vulnerability vs. fuzz-only artifact. The harness entry fuzz_one_input simply replaces normal Java callers with fuzz-driven data; the JNI method is a real app method (UbikeyJni.jGetKeyUsage) exported from libUbikeyJni.so and likely used at runtime. No evidence suggests fuzz-only stub code between JNI and CERT_Load; instead, it appears to be the actual production parsing path. Therefore, the crash likely maps directly to real inputs in the app context.

Because we saw __stack_chk_fail instead of a safe error return, the library is not just rejecting malformed certificates but actually corrupting its own stack frame. Once stack canaries are disabled or bypassed, this typically yields control-flow hijacking possibilities (e.g., overwrite return address) or at least a reliable crash (DoS). Given that the attacker can craft certificates, exploitability is at least theoretical and quite possibly practical depending on surrounding mitigations (ASLR, NX, etc.).

Without code, I cannot pin down the exact primitive (e.g., which field overflows), but the evidence clearly points to an out-of-bounds write derived from ASN.1 length fields. That aligns with CWE-787 (Out-of-bounds Write) and possibly CWE-121 (Stack-based Buffer Overflow). Since app is a banking app (kr.co.busanbank.mbp) and the library processes security credentials, the impact of a memory corruption in cert parsing via untrusted inputs is high.

I will classify this as a real vulnerability: is_vulnerable=true, with high severity and high but not absolute confidence (0.85) due to lack of direct decompilation yet strong circumstantial evidence from stack canary failure in parsing routine taking attacker-influenced input.
reasons: Stack canary failure (__stack_chk_fail) in Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT strongly indicates a stack-based buffer overflow or similar out-of-bounds write.

Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT and CERT_Load parse ASN.1/X.509 data that is realistically attacker-controlled (remote certificates or key material), and no evidence suggests the harness used impossible arguments.

The failing function is in libUbikeyJni.so and is called through a real JNI method (UbikeyJni.jGetKeyUsage) used by the banking app, so the vulnerable path is part of production code, not test-only code.
Failure occurs during parsing of malformed ASN.1, indicating that instead of safely rejecting bad input, the code corrupts its own stack, opening the door to DoS and potential control-flow hijacking.
recommendations: Review and harden ASN.1/X.509 parsing in Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT and related functions, ensuring all length fields and indices are bounds-checked before use.
Enable and run address and undefined-behavior sanitizers on libUbikeyJni.so with structured test cases (including malformed certificates) to pinpoint the exact overflow site.
Consider migrating certificate parsing to a well-maintained, audited library (e.g., BoringSSL/OpenSSL) or at minimum add robust defensive checks and fuzz testing around current code paths.
assumptions: UbikeyJni.jGetKeyUsage is reachable in production and processes externally supplied certificate/ASN.1 data (e.g., from networks or tokens).
The fuzz harness is not introducing impossible conditions (e.g., bypassing mandatory Java-side checks) beyond providing arbitrary ASN.1 blobs.
Stack protector was correctly configured, so __stack_chk_fail accurately signals stack frame corruption due to an out-of-bounds write.
limitations: Unable to decompile libUbikeyJni.so in this environment, so exact vulnerable line and buffer sizes cannot be shown.
No direct view of Java-side argument construction for UbikeyJni.jGetKeyUsage, so exact attacker control over each parameter is inferred from naming and typical use.
Exploitability level (beyond DoS) depends on runtime mitigations (stack canaries, ASLR, NX, SELinux) that are not fully known here.

exploit.trigger_method: Supply a crafted ASN.1/X.509 certificate or key blob that is parsed via UbikeyJni.jGetKeyUsage
prerequisites: Attacker can influence certificate or key data that the app processes (e.g., via a remote server, man-in-the-middle, or a connected token).
Target device runs a version of the app using the vulnerable libUbikeyJni.so without additional mitigations blocking exploitation.
Attacker can coerce the app to execute the jGetKeyUsage path on attacker-controlled ASN.1 data.
exploit_pipeline: Craft a malicious ASN.1/X.509 structure with length fields chosen to cause an out-of-bounds write in Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT while preserving overall parsing reachability.

Deliver the crafted certificate or key blob to the application through any interface that ultimately calls UbikeyJni.jGetKeyUsage (e.g., by presenting it as a server certificate or via a token/file input).
Trigger the code path that loads and parses this data (CERT_Load → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT).
Observe application crash due to __stack_chk_fail; with further refinement and knowledge of memory layout, attempt to shape the overflow to overwrite adjacent control data and gain code execution.
exploit.notes: Package is kr.co.busanbank.mbp; the exact activity and argument wiring to trigger UbikeyJni.jGetKeyUsage is unknown, so placeholders are used in PoC commands. A practical exploit would require understanding how the app ingests certificates and mapping that to the JNI call.

chain_of_thought: Identify first app-level native frame: Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum is the top frame and is an application function in libUbikeyJni.so, with deeper frames also within same lib. No allocator/sanitizer frames shown, so analysis starts there.

Given only symbolic stack and no disassembly, infer likely behavior from names: CERT_Load parses a certificate, Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT/TBS_CERT/NAME convert ASN.1 structures into internal X.509 representations, and Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum likely computes the number of children (sub-elements) in an ASN.1 structure. The crash reason is exactly at this function, suggesting either bounds miscalculation, invalid structure, or assertion/abort when structure malformed.

This kind of parsing logic (ASN.1, X.509) is highly sensitive to input length/structure. In production, the certificate input would be derived from external/untrusted sources (e.g., server certs, smartcard certs). In the fuzz setting, fuzz_one_input feeds arbitrary bytes into Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage, which then calls CERT_Load → Convert_* → Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum. That makes the ASN.1 tree directly driven by attacker-controlled bytes.

To classify as a vulnerability, we need evidence of unsafe behavior (e.g., out-of-bounds) vs. safe error handling (e.g., explicit termination on malformed ASN.1). The termination label 'Process Termination : Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum' is generic and does not mention malloc/free or sanitizer. It could be a deliberate abort() or explicit process termination when logical invariants are violated (e.g., unexpected child count) to avoid undefined behavior. Without code, we cannot see memcpy or pointer arithmetic, nor a sanitizer message indicating heap overflow, so we cannot firmly assert memory corruption.

The presence of a dedicated 'Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum' function suggests a logical parser routine; many custom ASN.1 stacks have historically had memory vulnerabilities due to trusting length fields. However, the provided information does not show a crash type that implies OOB (e.g., segfault, abort in malloc). It only says process termination at that function. That is compatible with a defensive check or assert that kills the process on invalid ASN.1, which is a controlled DoS but not necessarily a memory safety bug.

Since the harness fuzzes arbitrary input, malformed ASN.1 will be very common. A robust library would return a parse error. If instead it terminates the process in response to malformed input, this can be a denial-of-service condition. In the app context, remote input (e.g., certificate from NFC token or network handshake) could realistically trigger it. However, without knowing call sites or whether app exposes this parsing to untrusted parties, exploitability as DoS is uncertain. There is no direct indication of further impact like code execution.

Thus, there is clear input-driven crash behavior, but no strong evidence the crash is due to unsafe memory access; it may be intentional, defensive termination on invalid ASN.1. Given lack of allocator/sanitizer context and no disassembly, labeling this as a definite memory corruption vulnerability would be speculative. More conservatively, classify as non-confirmed vulnerability; at most a potential logic/DoS issue requiring further triage with code or crash logs. Under the requested binary classification, the evidence does not reach the threshold for 'vulnerability' as defined (no clear unsafe behavior).

Therefore, classify is_vulnerable=false with moderate-low confidence; note that further native analysis is recommended to check for unchecked length fields or out-of-bounds child index in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, but current data is insufficient to assert CWE-class memory corruption.
reasons: Crash occurs in ASN.1/X.509 parsing routine (Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum) with attacker-controlled input, but termination reason does not indicate memory corruption or allocator abort
Stack shows only application functions; no explicit evidence of out-of-bounds access, use-after-free, or unsafe memcpy at the crash site
Process termination could be an intentional abort/assert on malformed ASN.1 rather than undefined behavior, implying at most controlled DoS
Available data (no code, no detailed signal type) is insufficient to attribute crash to a concrete CWE memory-safety flaw
call_sequence: fuzz_one_input -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage -> CERT_Load -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_NAME -> Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum
recommendations: Obtain and review decompiled/native code for Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum and related ASN.1 parsing routines to check for unchecked length/child-count calculations
Instrument or run under ASan/Valgrind to determine whether malformed ASN.1 inputs cause actual memory errors versus intentional aborts
Add robust error handling for malformed ASN.1 (return error codes instead of aborting) and validate certificate inputs before parsing where feasible
assumptions: Certificate/ASN.1 data consumed by CERT_Load is at least partially attacker-controlled in realistic deployments
The reported 'Process Termination : Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum' is not accompanied by allocator or sanitizer diagnostics beyond what is shown
Fuzz harness passes raw, possibly malformed ASN.1 into the same parsing path used in production
limitations: No native disassembly or decompiled code for Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum and callers was available to inspect argument validation or bounds checks
Crash type (signal, abort message, sanitizer output) is not provided, limiting ability to map to specific CWE
No Java-side call graph or context beyond the single JNI bridge name, so real-world reachability and input sources are not fully known

chain_of_thought: Crash shows abort -> __stack_chk_fail -> ?? -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT, indicating a stack canary check failure (likely stack buffer overflow or corruption) inside Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT in libUbikeyJni.so.

Stack_chk_fail is raised by compiler-inserted stack protector when a local buffer (e.g., char buf[256]) or saved return address region is overwritten. That is almost always due to unsafe memory operation (memcpy, strcpy, etc.) or mis-sized local arrays based on input.

The fuzz harness entry is Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT, so the fuzzer is directly calling this function with attacker-controlled ASN.1 data. In normal app flow, Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT is likely called from Java via JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage with certificate or ASN.1 blob coming from user/server certificates, which are untrusted in cryptographic contexts.

Because Ghidra search_functions_by_name failed (tool HTTP 404), I cannot inspect the exact decompiled body. However, the function name strongly suggests ASN.1 parsing of X.509 TBS certificate, which is complex and commonly implemented via local parsing buffers and length fields taken from the ASN.1 input. Stack overflow or canary corruption during such parsing is classic evidence of out-of-bounds writes driven by malformed ASN.1 lengths or structure.

__stack_chk_fail is not triggered by harness misuse alone (e.g., passing NULL) but by overwriting the guard word on the stack. That implies a memory safety bug in app/native code, not a benign assertion. There is no evidence of custom abort or explicit abort() call before stack_chk_fail; the canary check is what aborted.

Attacker control: ASN.1 / certificate data is inherently attacker-controlled (comes from remote servers, NFC, smartcard, etc.). If this function is part of UbikeyJni (likely smartcard/PKI library), it will be fed with certificates from untrusted sources. With fuzzing already triggering overflow, a realistic attacker can craft inputs to reach same code path.

Even though we cannot see the exact memcpy call, the evidence supports a classic stack-based buffer overflow during ASN.1 to X509 conversion → out-of-bounds write on stack, detected by stack canary. This is a strong indicator of a real vulnerability, likely exploitable for RCE in native code, though ASLR/stack canaries increase difficulty.

Because the stack protector stopped the overflow early, exploitation is non-trivial but still theoretically feasible if an attacker finds a way to bypass/abuse partial corruption or other adjacent bugs (e.g., turning it into DoS or info leak). At minimum, this is a robust native crash on malformed input (= DoS) and at worst RCE. I will classify as high severity memory corruption vulnerability with practical exploitability potential.
reasons: Crash terminates via __stack_chk_fail, indicating stack canary corruption caused by out-of-bounds write in native code.
Crashing function Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT parses ASN.1/X.509 data, which is attacker-controllable and complex to parse safely.
Fuzzer directly calling Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT reproduces the issue, demonstrating malformed ASN.1 can trigger stack corruption.
Bug resides in app-provided library libUbikeyJni.so, not in harness-only glue or system allocator.
recommendations: Audit Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT implementation for all stack-allocated buffers and unsafe memory operations; replace with bounds-checked logic using ASN.1 length validation.
Add strict ASN.1 structural and length validation before parsing TBS certificate fields; reject inputs with inconsistent lengths or nested structures that would exceed buffer sizes.
Rebuild libUbikeyJni.so with full stack protections (stack canaries, PIE, RELRO, DEP) and consider using a well-vetted ASN.1/X.509 parsing library instead of custom code.
assumptions: Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT takes ASN.1-encoded certificate/TBS bytes whose length is influenced by attacker-controlled data.
Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage or its callers accept external certificates (e.g., from remote servers, files, or tokens) without fully constraining or sanitizing ASN.1 size/structure.
The observed __stack_chk_fail is due to a stack buffer overflow in application code rather than compiler or runtime anomaly.
limitations: Unable to decompile Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT due to tool errors, so the exact vulnerable line and buffer sizes are unknown.
No direct view of Java side (UbikeyJni.jGetKeyUsage) to confirm how inputs are constructed or whether there is any preprocessing.
Exploitability level (RCE vs. DoS) cannot be fully quantified without binary-level inspection and control-flow analysis.

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying a crafted ASN.1/X.509 certificate or key blob that is parsed via UbikeyJni.jGetKeyUsage, causing stack buffer overflow in Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT.
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to process an arbitrary or attacker-controlled certificate/ASN.1 blob (e.g., via network handshake, file import, or smartcard data).
Target device runs a version of the app containing the vulnerable libUbikeyJni.so.
Standard Android protections (ASLR, stack canaries) are present but can be bypassed in theory with further vulnerability research.
exploit_pipeline: Craft a malicious ASN.1/X.509 structure with manipulated length fields that cause Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT to write past a stack buffer while still following overall certificate format enough to reach deep parsing logic.

Deliver the crafted certificate to the app through a reachable feature that invokes UbikeyJni.jGetKeyUsage (e.g., importing a certificate file or initiating a handshake that the app validates natively).
Trigger the code path where the Java layer passes the ASN.1 data into the JNI bridge, which calls Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT internally.

On vulnerable builds, the malformed ASN.1 will corrupt the stack frame and hit __stack_chk_fail, causing the app to abort; with further refinement, partial corruption might be shaped toward hijacking control flow before the canary aborts.
exploit.notes: Exact app entrypoint and way to load certificates are unknown; PoC commands assume a feature that imports or verifies certificates by path and eventually calls UbikeyJni.jGetKeyUsage.

chain_of_thought: Stack trace only shows abort -> __stack_chk_fail with no symbolized application frames, indicating a stack canary check failed somewhere in libUbikeyJni.so or below, but exact function and instruction are unknown.

The first application-level native frame below allocators/sanitizers cannot be identified from the given frames (only abort and __stack_chk_fail plus unknown frames), so mandatory decompilation and backward data-flow from a concrete function cannot be performed.

The JNI bridge is Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage, but there is no Java callgraph, no decompiled JNI code, and no additional frames connecting this symbol to the crash site; we only know the library exposes this JNI, not that this specific call triggered the stack_chk_fail in the trace fragment.

__stack_chk_fail is typically invoked when a stack buffer overflow or similar stack corruption is detected, which often reflects a real memory safety issue; however, fuzz harnesses can also produce such conditions via unrealistic JNI argument patterns (e.g., passing NULL or violating expected invariants), especially when the internal code assumes well-formed parameters from higher-level logic.

Without any visibility into the native function body (no symbol, no address, no call parameters) and no Java-side path details, it is impossible to determine whether attacker-controllable input (user, network, file, IPC) can realistically influence the overflowing buffer or corrupt the stack under production conditions, versus this being an artifact of the fuzz harness or misconfiguration.

Given the lack of concrete code evidence, the correct classification is that a potential stack corruption occurred but cannot be substantiated as a genuine, reachable vulnerability in this app with the current data, so it should not be reported as a confirmed or likely vulnerability.

chain_of_thought: Ghidra MCP search by name failed with 404 responses, so I cannot decompile specific functions or inspect memcpy call sites; I must base assessment on the structured crash and general patterns.

The native stack shows a crash in __memcpy called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST → Get_DER_Child → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → CERT_Load → Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage, all inside libUbikeyJni.so, and the fuzz harness calls the JNI method directly, simulating Java usage.

These function names clearly indicate manual ASN.1/DER parsing and X.509 certificate conversion implemented in the app’s own library, not in a standard crypto library; memcpy-related crashes in parsers are very often due to length/offset mis-handling.

The fuzzer entry fuzz_one_input almost certainly feeds arbitrary, attacker-controlled DER/X.509 byte sequences into CERT_Load/Decode_DER_ASN1_ST via the JNI bridge, modeling untrusted certificate inputs (e.g., from smart card, server cert, or file).

Because __memcpy is the top crashing frame, the immediate failure is a memory safety violation (out-of-bounds read/write or overlap) rather than an intentional logic abort; memcpy normally does not crash unless the pointers/length are invalid.

Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and Get_DER_Child strongly suggest operations like copying child TLV contents based on ASN.1 length fields and offsets; if these fields are derived from untrusted DER data without robust bounds checks, malformed inputs can produce an oversized length fed to memcpy.

There’s no indication this is a harness-only artifact like passing a NULL env or invalid JNI objects; the stack is a clean decode path and there are no sanitizer frames, abort(), or obvious harness stubs in the trace.

Given no evidence of systematic bounds checks and the generic naming (custom DER parser), the most reasonable interpretation is that memcpy length and/or source/dest pointers are ultimately derived from attacker-supplied DER length/offset fields, and that some malformed combination causes memcpy to crash.

This corresponds to a classic out-of-bounds access / buffer overflow in a certificate parser, which is generally exploitable in principle (heap or stack corruption), although exploitability details (stack vs heap, ASLR, exact overwrite) are unknown without disassembly.

Reachability in the real app is plausible: jGetKeyUsage probably loads a certificate via CERT_Load and parses it to extract key-usage bits, likely from an external token/smart card or server-supplied cert; those certificates can be controlled or at least influenced in many threat models.

Given the limited tooling visibility, I cannot show exact variables or code snippets, but the combination of memcpy crash, ASN.1 length-based helpers, and fuzz-driven repro provides strong circumstantial evidence of a genuine memory-corruption bug rather than a harness artifact.

Therefore I classify this as a likely real vulnerability (out-of-bounds memcpy due to unvalidated ASN.1 length), with high but not absolute confidence and high severity; exploitability is at least theoretical and potentially practical if the attacker can supply crafted certificate data.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy during custom ASN.1/DER and X.509 parsing in libUbikeyJni.so, consistent with unsafe buffer copying based on parsed lengths.
Function names Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and Get_DER_Child indicate manual handling of DER child elements and length/offset fields, which are typically attacker-controlled in certificate inputs.
No evidence of harness-only misuse (e.g., invalid JNI env or deliberate NULLs); stack is a normal decode path from Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage through CERT_Load.
Memcpy crashing implies invalid pointers/lengths, which here are almost certainly derived from malformed DER data provided by the fuzzer, modeling untrusted certificate inputs.
Such a flaw corresponds to classic out-of-bounds read/write in a parser and is commonly exploitable, at least for denial-of-service and potentially for memory corruption based attacks.
recommendations: Disassemble and review Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, Get_DER_Child, and related helpers to locate all memcpy/memmove calls and ensure all lengths and offsets are validated against buffer boundaries.
Introduce strict bounds checking on all ASN.1 length fields and child offsets before performing buffer copies or allocations; reject frames with inconsistent or oversized lengths.
Consider replacing custom ASN.1/X.509 parsing with a well-tested library (e.g., BoringSSL/OpenSSL) or at minimum add extensive fuzzing with ASan/UBSan enabled to verify fixes.
assumptions: The fuzzer models realistic flows where untrusted certificate/DER data is passed into jGetKeyUsage via CERT_Load.
ASN.1 length and offset values used in memcpy calls are at least partially influenced by attacker-controlled input.
There is no hidden safety wrapper around __memcpy that intentionally aborts on benign conditions (unlikely given naming).
limitations: Could not decompile functions due to MCP server 404 errors, so specific variables and code paths are inferred from naming and crash stack only.
Exact nature of the memcpy misuse (out-of-bounds read vs write, heap vs stack) is unknown, so exploitability is characterized at a high level only.
Java-side call graph into jGetKeyUsage and actual external entry points (e.g., activities, services) could not be inspected, so real-world reachability depends on app logic.

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying a crafted X.509/DER certificate or key blob that is parsed via jGetKeyUsage/CERT_Load
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to parse attacker-controlled or at least partially controlled DER/X.509 data (e.g., via smart card, file import, or network).
The vulnerable parsing code and memcpy call are present and reachable in the deployed build.
Typical platform mitigations (ASLR, NX) are in place, so exploitation may require careful heap shaping and multiple attempts.
exploit_pipeline: Identify an app feature that calls UbikeyJni.jGetKeyUsage with certificate or key material loaded from an external source (file, token, server).
Use differential fuzzing on that feature to derive DER/X.509 payloads that cause the same memcpy crash observed in the native fuzzer.
Refine payload lengths and structure (e.g., child element sizes in ASN.1 sequences) to control the memcpy length and destination so that controlled data overwrites adjacent heap or stack metadata.
Leverage resulting memory corruption to hijack control flow or at minimum cause a reliable denial-of-service when the vulnerable parsing path is triggered in the app.
exploit.notes: Package and activity names are unknown; replace <PACKAGE>/<ACTIVITY> with the actual entry point that loads certificates and ultimately calls UbikeyJni.jGetKeyUsage. The PoC DER file should mimic the structure of a normal certificate but use malformed length and child element fields to reproduce the memcpy crash.

chain_of_thought: Top native frame before libc is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST in libUbikeyJni.so, followed by CERT_Load and the JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage. The crash happens in __memcpy, which indicates an out-of-bounds copy or invalid pointer, but I cannot inspect the implementation because Ghidra MCP search for these functions failed (404 / missing binary context).

Given the naming, Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and CERT_Load strongly suggest certificate/ASN.1 parsing code operating on DER-encoded input. memcpy inside such parsing routines is a classic place for length/offset miscalculations leading to buffer overflows or overreads if length fields from the ASN.1 structure are not fully validated against the actual buffer size.

The fuzz harness entry is fuzz_one_input, which likely feeds arbitrary data to the JNI bridge. However, in the real app, the data being parsed as a certificate is very likely to ultimately derive from untrusted sources (server certificates, smartcard/USB tokens, QR codes, etc.). Thus, the general code path of "parse DER certificate via Decode_DER_ASN1_ST" is plausibly reachable with attacker-controlled inputs in production usage, even though the exact wiring from the Java side is not visible here.

Because there is a direct crash at __memcpy during DER/ASN.1 decode, and there is no evidence this is caused by the harness doing something structurally impossible (e.g., passing null instead of real byte arrays, wrong JNI ownership expectations), the most natural explanation is that Decode_DER_ASN1_ST (or CERT_Load) computed an incorrect length or pointer and called memcpy without proper bounds checking. That is a memory safety bug in the library itself, not in the harness.

Without decompiled code, I can't confirm the exact expressions for src/dst/len or their validations, so I cannot be 0.9+ confident, but the combination of function names, stack structure, and allocator-level memcpy crash in a parser function is very strong circumstantial evidence for an out-of-bounds read/write caused by attacker-influenced certificate data.

Thus I classify this as a likely genuine vulnerability (CWE-787 / CWE-119 style buffer overflow in ASN.1/DER certificate parsing), potentially exploitable for memory corruption and at least a reliable DoS via malformed certificate inputs. Confidence is moderate-high (~0.75) but not certainty due to missing binary and Java code context.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST/CERT_Load, strongly indicating unsafe memory copy during ASN.1/DER parsing
Certificate/ASN.1 parsing typically processes attacker-controlled DER data from remote or external sources; malformed inputs can realistically reach this path
No evidence the crash is due solely to JNI harness misuse (e.g., impossible nulls); stack shows a normal JNI → CERT_Load → Decode_DER_ASN1_ST → memcpy flow
The nature of the failure (process termination in memcpy) and function naming are consistent with an out-of-bounds access or invalid pointer derived from unvalidated length fields
recommendations: Obtain and review the implementation of Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and CERT_Load, focusing on memcpy/memmove calls and how lengths and offsets are computed from DER/ASN.1 fields
Add strict bounds checks ensuring all parsed lengths are validated against the total buffer size before any memory copy; use safe wrappers that abort or return error on size mismatch
Augment tests/fuzzing specifically for CERT_Load/Decode_DER_ASN1_ST with address/UB sanitizers enabled to reproduce and localize the exact out-of-bounds condition
assumptions: Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and CERT_Load are genuine ASN.1/DER certificate parsers used in production code paths
Certificate/DER data eventually originates from an attacker-controllable or semi-trusted source (e.g., network, token) and can be malformed
The fuzz harness approximates realistic use by passing variable-length DER blobs into the JNI method rather than violating basic JNI invariants
limitations: Could not decompile or inspect libUbikeyJni.so due to tool errors (404 / missing binary), so memcpy argument derivation and validation logic are inferred from names and crash site only
Java-side call graph details and how inputs are constructed/validated before calling jGetKeyUsage are not available
No exact crash register/memory state or sanitizer diagnostics to distinguish between OOB read vs write or null/poisoned pointer

exploit.trigger_method: Malformed DER/ASN.1 certificate data passed through Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to process attacker-supplied or modified DER-encoded certificate/KeyUsage data
Vulnerable libUbikeyJni.so is present and the JNI method jGetKeyUsage is reachable in the deployed configuration
No additional robust validation is performed in Java before passing the data to the native CERT_Load/Decode_DER_ASN1_ST functions
exploit_pipeline: Craft a DER/ASN.1 certificate or structure with inconsistent length fields designed to cause Decode_DER_ASN1_ST to compute a length larger than the actual buffer or to miscalculate an offset

Deliver this malformed certificate to the app via whatever mechanism it uses for certificates (e.g., TLS handshake, imported certificate file, smart card/token interaction) so that it is ultimately passed to UbikeyJni.jGetKeyUsage
When the app calls Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage, CERT_Load invokes Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, which then calls memcpy with attacker-influenced length/offset
The oversized or misaligned memcpy triggers an out-of-bounds memory access, leading to process crash (DoS) and, in some environments, potential memory corruption that could be leveraged further
exploit.notes: Exact Java components and file-intent wiring are unknown; the PoC commands assume there is an Activity that can load a user-supplied certificate and ultimately call UbikeyJni.jGetKeyUsage. A direct instrumentation-based PoC could instead invoke the JNI method from a test harness within the app process using the same DER payload.
